techniques.evidence_quote: a Drizly employee posted company cloud computing account login information on the software development and hosting platform GitHub
hackers were able to use Drizly's servers to mine cryptocurrency until the company changed its login information for its cloud computing account

attack_chain.description: A Drizly employee committed AWS cloud computing account login credentials to a public GitHub repository, exposing them to attackers.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: In 2018, a Drizly employee posted company cloud computing account login information on the software development and hosting platform GitHub.

evidence_chunks.quote: In 2018, a Drizly employee posted company cloud computing account login information on the software development and hosting platform GitHub.
In 2018, a Drizly employee posted company cloud computing account login information on the software development and hosting platform GitHub.

attack_chain.description: Attackers harvested the leaked AWS credentials from GitHub and authenticated to Drizly's cloud computing account.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: hackers were able to use Drizly's servers

attack_chain.description: Attackers abused Drizly's AWS compute resources to mine cryptocurrency until Drizly rotated the compromised credentials.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: hackers were able to use Drizly's servers to mine cryptocurrency until the company changed its login information for its cloud computing account

initial_access_vector: AWS cloud account login credentials were posted to a public GitHub repository by a Drizly employee and harvested by attackers.
persistence_mechanism: Continued use of the leaked valid AWS credentials until Drizly rotated them.
privilege_escalation: None described; attackers used the valid credentials directly.
